title: Changing a Flat Tyre
description: The learner must walk through the complete procedure for safely changing a flat tyre on the roadside.
situation: You are driving on a motorway when you notice the car is pulling to one side and handling oddly. You pull over to the hard shoulder and discover you have a flat tyre. Your spare tyre and jack are in the boot. Walk through exactly what you would do — step by step, in as much detail as you can. When you feel you've said everything, click 'I'm Done' to continue.
constraints: The car must be parked on level ground away from traffic before jacking
The handbrake must be applied before any wheel is removed
Wheel nuts must be tightened in a diagonal pattern and to the manufacturer's specification

expert_answers.answer: 1. Signal and move to the hard shoulder; switch on hazard lights. 2. Apply the handbrake and switch off the engine. 3. Get all occupants out of the vehicle and behind the barrier or well away from traffic. 4. Retrieve the spare tyre, jack, wheel brace, and locking wheel-nut key from the boot. 5. Place warning triangle at least 45 metres behind the car. 6. Loosen the wheel nuts on the flat tyre slightly while the wheel is still on the ground (break torque). 7. Consult the vehicle handbook to locate the correct jack point for that corner. 8. Position the jack under the correct jacking point and raise the car until the flat tyre clears the ground. 9. Remove the wheel nuts completely and keep them safe. 10. Remove the flat tyre and mount the spare, aligning holes to studs. 11. Hand-tighten the wheel nuts in a diagonal/star pattern. 12. Lower the car fully until the jack is no longer bearing weight. 13. Tighten the wheel nuts fully in diagonal order to the recommended torque. 14. Stow the flat tyre, jack, and equipment securely. 15. Check the spare tyre pressure at the earliest opportunity. 16. Observe any speed restriction on the spare (typically 50 mph for a space-saver). 17. Have the punctured tyre inspected or replaced as soon as possible.

decision_points: Is the car in a safe position to jack — level, firm ground, away from traffic?
Is the spare a full-size spare or a space-saver, and does this change any steps?
Is there a locking wheel-nut key, and do you have it before raising the car?
failure_modes: Loosening wheel nuts after raising the car (wheel spins freely; car may fall off jack)
Jacking on the wrong bodywork point (risk of chassis damage or jack collapse)
Not applying the handbrake before jacking (car can roll off jack)
Overtightening or undertightening wheel nuts by hand without final torque check
Leaving passengers in the car while working on the hard shoulder
Forgetting to stow the flat tyre and equipment before driving off
edge_cases: The car has run-flat tyres — these may not need an immediate tyre change but must be inspected
There is no spare tyre — must call breakdown assistance instead
The flat is on a live motorway lane — never change a tyre there; call for assistance
The jack is damaged or missing — do not attempt to change; call for assistance
Another tyre is also low on pressure — check all tyres before driving off

probe_bank.probe_text: You mentioned loosening the wheel nuts — at exactly what point in the sequence do you do that, and why does the order matter?
probe_bank.success_criteria: Learner explains that nuts must be loosened while the tyre is on the ground so the wheel cannot spin freely, and that doing it after jacking risks destabilising the car.

probe_bank.probe_text: How do you know where to position the jack under the car without damaging it?
probe_bank.success_criteria: Learner mentions consulting the vehicle handbook or owner's manual, or describes locating the manufacturer-designated reinforced jack point on the sill or chassis.

probe_bank.probe_text: Why do you tighten the wheel nuts in a diagonal pattern rather than going around the wheel in order?
probe_bank.success_criteria: Learner explains that diagonal (star) tightening ensures even seating of the wheel against the hub, preventing warping or uneven clamping force.

probe_bank.probe_text: How would your approach change if the spare in the boot is a compact space-saver rather than a full-size spare?
probe_bank.success_criteria: Learner identifies the speed restriction (typically 50 mph / 80 km/h), the need to replace or repair the tyre as soon as possible, and any handling limitations.

probe_bank.probe_text: What could go wrong if you raised the car on the jack before loosening the wheel nuts?
probe_bank.success_criteria: Learner explains that the wheel spins freely once off the ground, making loosening impossible, and that the torque applied could destabilise the car on the jack.

probe_bank.probe_text: What would you do differently if you discovered the flat tyre while on a live motorway lane rather than the hard shoulder?
probe_bank.success_criteria: Learner states they would NOT attempt to change the tyre on a live lane, would call emergency services or breakdown assistance, keep occupants in the car with seatbelts on, and activate hazard lights.
